behaviors.description: Rows with missing required fields are rejected and appear in the dead-letter queue with the original row number.
behaviors.justification: This behavior is about an externally visible side effect (row is actually written to the dead-letter queue) plus metadata preservation (original row number). Per ROUND-TRIP EFFECT RULE and SYSTEM TRIAD (prove external side effect via readback), a system test is needed. Integration was considered but rejected because a mocked queue could hide serialization/routing/producer issues that only appear on the real queue boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Run the pipeline against a real test bucket/object and real DLQ endpoint, include rows missing required fields, then consume/read back DLQ messages and assert each rejected row includes the correct original CSV row number.

behaviors.description: The transformation correctly converts date strings in multiple formats (ISO 8601, US, European) to a single canonical format.
behaviors.justification: This is deterministic transformation logic (format parsing/normalization) and does not require OS/network/subprocess or model output. Higher levels were considered and rejected: the behavior text is specifically about converting date strings to canonical format, not about external I/O boundaries.
behaviors.test_description: Feed representative ISO 8601, US, and European date strings into the transformer and assert canonical outputs (including edge cases like ambiguous day/month and invalid dates).

behaviors.description: A CSV file with 500,000 rows completes processing within the configured timeout without running out of memory.
behaviors.justification: This is a performance/resource behavior tied to real runtime characteristics (memory pressure, timeout under actual process execution). Mocks cannot reproduce true memory behavior or end-to-end throughput constraints reliably, so System is required.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the pipeline end-to-end with a 500k-row fixture in an environment matching production limits, measure wall-clock duration vs configured timeout, and assert process memory stays below limits (no OOM/kill).

behaviors.description: When the S3 bucket returns a transient 503 error, the pipeline retries with exponential backoff rather than failing the entire batch.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode depends on real external error-path behavior (S3/HTTP 503 handling) and retry timing semantics at the network boundary. Integration was considered but rejected because stubbed responses can miss real SDK/HTTP classification and retry interaction details; this matches SYSTEM TRIAD error-path verification.
behaviors.test_description: Against a real or fault-injected S3-compatible endpoint that emits transient 503s, run ingestion and assert retries occur with increasing backoff intervals and the batch eventually continues instead of terminal failure.

behaviors.description: Rows successfully written to PostgreSQL are not duplicated if the pipeline is restarted mid-batch.
behaviors.justification: This requires proving durability/idempotency across process restart and database boundary via round-trip readback. It involves OS/process behavior (restart) plus external side-effect verification in PostgreSQL, which exceeds integration-only confidence.
behaviors.test_description: Start a batch on real PostgreSQL, force a restart after partial progress, resume processing, then query the table and assert exactly-once outcomes (no duplicates for already committed rows).
